executiveSummary:
**Executive Summary: Medicaid Program Performance Analysis**

National Medicaid enrollment stands at 75.3 million beneficiaries as of January 2026, with most large states experiencing enrollment declines following the end of the public health emergency continuous coverage provisions. California leads with 12.6 million enrollees but shows the steepest year-over-year decline at -6.2%, while Michigan (-7.6%) and Illinois (-6.2%) also demonstrate significant reductions. Texas maintains 4.0 million enrollees with a more moderate -3.1% decline, positioning it as the third-largest state program. Notably, North Carolina stands as an outlier with positive growth at +0.5%, likely reflecting recent Medicaid expansion implementation. Total national expenditures reached $215.9 billion in fiscal year 2024, with a national per-enrollee average of $2,869.

Cost efficiency analysis reveals substantial interstate variation, with per-enrollee spending ranging from Florida's $2,031 to Pennsylvania's $4,377. Texas demonstrates competitive cost management at $2,988 per enrollee, falling slightly above the national average but significantly below high-cost states like Pennsylvania ($4,377) and Massachusetts ($4,201). However, quality performance presents areas for improvement, with Texas scoring 62/100 and ranking 30th nationally—trailing regional peers and high-performing states like Massachusetts (81) and Minnesota (80). Texas's managed care penetration of 93.3% exceeds the national average of 83.2%, suggesting strong infrastructure for care coordination that could be leveraged to improve quality outcomes. Current regulatory activity indicates increased federal focus on home and community-based services quality measures for 2028, presenting an opportunity for proactive quality enhancement initiatives, particularly given Texas's moderate quality tier status and the gap between its cost efficiency and quality performance.
texasSpotlight:
## Texas Medicaid Program Performance Analysis

Texas operates the nation's third-largest Medicaid program with 4.03 million enrollees, representing a substantial 5.4% of national enrollment despite serving a state with nearly 9% of the U.S. population. This enrollment gap reflects Texas's continued resistance to Medicaid expansion, limiting coverage primarily to children, pregnant women, and disabled individuals. The state's -3.1% year-over-year enrollment decline mirrors the national trend as pandemic-era continuous enrollment policies unwind, performing similarly to peer non-expansion states like Florida (-4.0%) but better than expansion states experiencing steeper drops like Michigan (-7.6%). At $2,988 per enrollee, Texas spends 4% above the national average of $2,869, positioning it between low-cost southern peers like North Carolina ($2,616) and Florida ($2,031), while remaining well below expansion states like Pennsylvania ($4,377) and Ohio ($3,561) that cover broader, more costly adult populations.

Texas demonstrates operational strengths through its robust managed care infrastructure, with 93.3% of beneficiaries enrolled in managed care plans—significantly exceeding the 83.2% national average and reflecting the state's early adoption and sophisticated implementation of risk-based contracting. However, quality outcomes present a more mixed picture, with Texas scoring 62/100 on CMS quality measures, ranking 30th nationally in the "moderate" performance tier. While this exceeds several southern peers like Arkansas (54) and all bottom-five performers, it falls short of high-performing states and suggests room for improvement in care coordination and health outcomes. The combination of high managed care penetration with moderate quality scores indicates that while Texas has built effective delivery system infrastructure, translating that operational capacity into superior health outcomes remains a key challenge. Areas to monitor include whether the state's quality scores improve as post-pandemic enrollment stabilizes and whether ongoing federal oversight activities signal emerging compliance or performance concerns.

alerts.title: Pennsylvania Per-Enrollee Spending 53% Above National Average
alerts.description: Pennsylvania's per-enrollee spending of $4,377 is significantly higher than the national average of $2,869, indicating potential cost control issues.

alerts.title: Florida Unusually Low Per-Enrollee Spending
alerts.description: Florida's per-enrollee spending of $2,031 is 29% below the national average, which may indicate restrictive benefits or efficient operations.

alerts.title: Bottom 5 States Show Critical Quality Performance Gap
alerts.description: Five states (MS, OK, AL, SC, AR) have quality scores below 55, representing a 17+ point gap from the national average of 65.6.

alerts.title: Massachusetts Combines High Quality with High Spending
alerts.description: Massachusetts achieves top-tier quality scores (81/100) while spending $4,201 per enrollee, suggesting high investment in quality care.

alerts.title: Texas High Managed Care Penetration Above National Average
alerts.description: Texas managed care penetration of 93.3% exceeds the national average of 83.2% by 10 percentage points, indicating strong managed care adoption.

executiveInsights.title: Texas quality score at risk tier with national ranking decline
executiveInsights.summary: Texas Medicaid quality score is 62/100, ranking #30 of 50 states in moderate tier. This places Texas just 2 points above the low performer threshold of <60.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Quality scores directly impact federal funding and contract renewals. Being near the low performer threshold creates reputational and financial risk for both HHSC and Accenture.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Flag quality improvement initiatives to HHSC leadership immediately

executiveInsights.title: Texas managed care penetration 10% above national average
executiveInsights.summary: Texas has 93.3% managed care penetration versus 83.2% national average, with 3.86M MC enrollees. This represents one of the highest penetration rates nationally.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: High MC penetration indicates strong program maturity but also suggests limited room for traditional expansion. Accenture should focus on optimization and value-based care initiatives rather than basic MC transition.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review managed care optimization opportunities with Texas HHSC

executiveInsights.title: Texas enrollment declining at 3.1% while North Carolina grows
executiveInsights.summary: Texas lost 3.1% enrollment YoY (4.03M total) while peer state North Carolina gained 0.5%. Texas decline mirrors national trend but NC shows growth is possible.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Enrollment decline affects contract value and may signal operational issues. NC's growth provides a benchmark for potential interventions and best practices to propose to HHSC.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Analyze North Carolina enrollment growth strategies for Texas application

executiveInsights.title: New HCBS quality measures for 2028 require immediate planning
executiveInsights.summary: CMS announced 2028 Medicaid Home and Community-Based Services Quality Measure Set through Federal Register. New requirements will impact Texas HCBS programs.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: HCBS represents significant portion of Medicaid spend and Texas already has quality challenges. Early preparation for 2028 measures could provide competitive advantage and improve outcomes.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Review 2028 HCBS quality measures with Texas HHSC compliance team

executiveInsights.title: Texas per-enrollee costs $120 above national average
executiveInsights.summary: Texas spends $2,988 per enrollee versus national average of $2,869. Total Texas expenditure is $12.0B with 4.03M enrollees.
executiveInsights.whyItMatters: Higher per-enrollee costs combined with declining enrollment and moderate quality scores suggests potential efficiency opportunities. This creates opening for Accenture to propose cost optimization initiatives.
executiveInsights.actionPrompt: Flag cost efficiency analysis opportunities to HHSC finance team

riskOpportunity.label: Quality measure gap
riskOpportunity.description: Texas ranks #30 nationally with 62/100 quality score, creating vulnerability in performance-based contracts and CMS oversight.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Deploy quality improvement accelerator program targeting specific low-performing measures.

riskOpportunity.label: Enrollment decline pressure
riskOpportunity.description: Texas Medicaid enrollment dropped 3.1% YoY, reducing program scale and potentially triggering budget adjustments.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Develop enrollment stabilization strategy focusing on retention and eligible population outreach.

riskOpportunity.label: HCBS quality expansion
riskOpportunity.description: New 2028 HCBS quality measures create major service delivery transformation opportunity for managed care partners.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Position as lead HCBS transformation partner by developing comprehensive compliance and delivery framework.

riskOpportunity.label: OIG oversight intensification
riskOpportunity.description: Active HHS OIG Medicaid portfolio and work plan items signal heightened compliance scrutiny across state programs.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Implement proactive compliance monitoring system with real-time risk detection capabilities.

riskOpportunity.label: Procurement window opening
riskOpportunity.description: Active Texas HHSC procurement opportunities and ESBD portal activity indicate potential contract recompetes or expansions.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Establish dedicated procurement intelligence team to monitor and rapidly respond to emerging opportunities.

riskOpportunity.label: Managed care dominance
riskOpportunity.description: Texas 93.3% MC penetration (vs 83.2% national) creates concentrated partnership opportunities but also competitive risk.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Strengthen relationships with top 3 Texas MCOs while developing contingency partnerships.

riskOpportunity.label: Cost efficiency advantage
riskOpportunity.description: Texas per-enrollee costs of $2,988 are below national average of $2,869, demonstrating operational efficiency potential.
riskOpportunity.recommendedAction: Leverage Texas cost efficiency model as template for expansion to other high-cost state programs.

pulseMetrics.label: Total Enrollment
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas Medicaid serves 4,032,864 enrollees as of January 2026.

pulseMetrics.label: 12-Mo Enrollment Delta
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas enrollment declined 3.1% year-over-year, slightly better than the national trend.

pulseMetrics.label: Per Enrollee Spend
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas spends $2,988 per Medicaid enrollee annually, below the national average of $2,869.

pulseMetrics.label: Managed Care Rate
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas has 93.3% managed care penetration with 3,863,808 enrollees in managed care plans.

pulseMetrics.label: Quality Score
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Texas ranks #30 of 50 states with a quality score of 62, classified as moderate performance.

pulseMetrics.label: Active Procurement Items
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Two active Texas procurement portals are being monitored for Medicaid-related opportunities.

pulseMetrics.label: OIG/Regulatory Signals
pulseMetrics.tooltip: Six regulatory and oversight items are currently active in federal monitoring systems.

pulseMetrics.label: Next Milestone
pulseMetrics.tooltip: No specific upcoming milestones are identified in the current data.
